lines.text: Human writing has taken dozens of independent forms over five millennia. Each script
reflects a specific relationship between sound, symbol, and direction. This document
exercises the engine across the major script families: Latin, CJK ideographs, Arabic
abjad, Thai abugida, Hangul syllabic blocks, and the combining-mark structures that
Acute, grave, and circumflex accents attach as zero-width combining marks above their
base letters: á ë ô ñ ṣ. Stacked forms arise when two accents modify a single glyph: ñ́.
Each of these sequences must be measured as a single grapheme cluster — splitting
on a combining mark boundary is a rendering error.
East European and Vietnamese extensions cover a wider range of diacritics: ă ắ ặ ổ ẽ ư
ứ ự. Extended Latin-B adds historical and phonetic forms: ȅ ȃ ȏ ȗ — all of them within
the Arimo and Noto Sans coverage area.
(Hànzì) — Chinese characters used for Mandarin, Cantonese, and classical texts:
天地玄黄，宇宙洪荒。日月盈昃，辰宿列张。寒来暑往，秋收冬藏。闰余成岁，律吕调阳。
(Nihongo) — Japanese mixes kanji with hiragana and katakana syllabaries:
(Hangugeo) — Korean Hangul syllable blocks, each composed from jamo
Variation selectors change glyph form without changing Unicode identity:
variation-selector sequence. Such sequences must not be split across segments.
Mixing scripts in a single paragraph — a common requirement in multilingual publishing:
must resolve direction per-run and align glyphs from the correct edge.
— Thai is written left to right without spaces between words. Vowel signs
appear above, below, before, and after their consonant base:
. Each cluster must be measured as one
สวัสดีครับ
— A standard Thai greeting, usefully representative of the typical glyph-stack
("thank you very much") follows: every syllable carries at
least one diacritic mark above or below the base consonant.
This paragraph forces wraps while mixing scripts in high density: English +
Zero-width joiners and variation sequences at segment boundaries: ☺‍☻, ♀‍♂,
wrapped in text. Each joined sequence must survive line-breaking without being torn
apart at the ZWJ or VS codepoint.
Combining marks that stack: q̈ (q + combining diaeresis), Z̧ (Z + combining cedilla
below), ẇ (w + combining dot above). These test that grapheme width is measured on
the composed form, not the base character alone.
Unicode Standard §3.6: A grapheme cluster is an approximation to what a user
considers to be a character. The engine must treat each cluster as atomic for
